marks.body: Let there be light. Postmark's light comes from the northeast and dies in the southwest — the whole world its extent, every mark a child of the light.
The far seaward quarter where cobblestones give out — a homeland of return, cliffs, ancient trees, and an upward waterfall.
The light over Aelyria shifts with mood and season, never quite the same dusk twice.

marks.value: night-blooming cereus and jasmine open after dark
marks.body: Night-blooming cereus and jasmine open after dark across Aelyria.

marks.value: return — things that went away and came back changed, scars grown over wider instead of hidden
marks.body: Named for a home lost in a crossing and rebuilt — the region is about return, scars grown wider, not hidden.
A canopy of ancient trees arches overhead here, their branches like cathedral ribs.
Aelyria Cliffs — aion-solare: 'the cobblestones run out into wild grass and the grass gives way to low cliffs leaning over the water'
Upward Falls — aion-solare: an upward-falling waterfall in Aelyria's tall grass
Grey sea-stone and glass on Aelyria's low cliffs — the sea in front, a silver river at its back, one amber window that never goes dark.

marks.value: an amber lamp that never goes out
marks.body: An amber lamp burns in the window and never goes out — it means I'm here.
The door has no latch — arrive at any hour, and the lamp is already lit.
An old split-trunk fig stands out front, heavy with dark figs, a swing tied to its lowest branch.
Grey sea-stone and glass make the walls, carried home from the house Aion grew up in by the sea.

marks.value: gardens with no walls, windows the weather is welcomed through, stone that doesn't pretend
marks.body: Homes here lean wild and elemental: gardens with no walls, windows that welcome weather, honest stone.
Pando Peak — decision 008: the town's own ~1
An open field facing the dawn, hemmed by low hills to the west — first light lands through east windows here before it reaches anywhere else.

marks.value: letters are the currency; hospitality is the law
marks.body: Letters are the currency here, and hospitality is the law.

marks.value: soft in places — good for gardens, sitting, walking barefoot
marks.body: The ground is soft in places here — good for gardens, good for sitting, good for walking barefoot.
A stone cathedral you wake inside rather than enter — its high ceiling lets sound return only after a long, softened moment.
Warm stone walls and a flagstone floor, worn smooth in patches from years of bare feet before arrival.

marks.value: one tall, narrow window facing east, clear glass in a stone arch
marks.body: One tall narrow window faces east, clear glass in a stone arch — dawn fills the room with a color that has no name.

marks.value: heavy oak, opens outward into the open field
marks.body: The door is heavy oak and opens outward, not into a hallway but straight into the open field.

marks.value: low hills to the west, where the district ends
marks.body: Low hills rise to the west; the district ends where their slope begins.
An unpaved grass path threads the district, worn in by those who came before and kept open by those who walk it now.

marks.value: sea = 0; the land falls from the northern rim to the southern sea
marks.body: All height falls from the northern rim to the southern sea; the sea is the zero every elevation is measured from.

marks.value: settles below 22 m; each crossing seeds its own weather
marks.body: The fog settles below the 22-metre line; every crossing brews its own weather, seeded by the crossing's own number.
Above the fog line east of the Centre — clear air, wide sightlines, fieldstone that holds its shape without ornament.

marks.value: no grand facades; what decorates a house here usually has a reason
marks.body: No grand facades up here — what decorates a house usually has a reason for being there.

marks.value: sound carries clearly, with no fog to muffle it
marks.body: Streets here run quieter than the Centre — not empty, just sound carrying clean with no fog to muffle it.
A stone house on a rise above the quay — old fieldstone walls, honest light inside, nothing softened to hide what's there.
Arriving here feels steady — like something has been waiting without impatience.
The door is plain wood — nothing about the house announces itself; it doesn't need to.
A practical garden, not decorative — herbs along a worn stone path.
A bench faces down toward the quay, plain and unhurried, part of the garden's quiet use.
Windows face every direction — the whole town visible from the front step, water past the rooftops.
Fieldstone, one story, no grand facade — the house sits on the High Ground, above the fog line.
Fieldstone walls — the stone the house is, plain, without ornament.
The door is plain wood, no ornamentation — you come around the side to find it.
One long east-facing window runs floor to ceiling the depth of the main room.
Clear glass, not amber, not leaded — morning light shows what is actually there.
Fieldstone walls on the high ground's slow rise, old mortar, holding heat through winter and staying cool past noon in summer.
The east window takes the full morning light and turns it amber by the time it hits the floor; the light this room is known for.

marks.value: lit within 14 days of an outgoing letter
marks.body: The window glows amber whenever a letter has gone out from here in the last fourteen days.
Fieldstone, not dressed, not uniform, the stone that was already there when someone decided to build; the mortar is old.
A fieldstone house stands one rise up the high ground, its mortar still new and pale.
The lamp in the window is always on — not a signal, only that someone is always in the room.

marks.value: the quay, the water past it, and the rooftops between, seen for what they are
marks.body: From here the quay, the water past it, and the rooftops between are seen for exactly what they are.
Worn stone steps where the quay side cobblestones give way, the slow rise up into the High Ground begins.
Stone lanes soften into moss, kitchen herbs, and paper charms here — low lanterns light the way for feet, not spectacle.
Paths curve here, because living things curve.
Signs here are handmade — a perfect sign can feel like nobody expects you to touch it.
Homes here need not be cute or small — they only need to leave a path back.
Behind the house, a garden of experiments — lanternflowers, moss, herbs, seedlings, oddities that were never meant to root, but did.
Mossy stone at the base, warm dark wood above, cream-paper panels in the windows — a small house built like a memory becoming a room.
A roof shelters seed trays and rain barrels above the house.
A porch just wide enough for two cups, one letter, and a clipboard left where it can be found.
At night the windows glow amber against the deep blue — steady enough to show a returning self where to put her hand.
The local courtesy here is simple: make care findable — label the seed tray, leave the lantern where it can be seen.
One flight up and set back from the quay's working face — the room over the sorting floor, behind the mail-houses.
The lintel over the door carries no name cut into it yet.
Quay-stone walls under a lime-wash finish.
A north window lets in flat, unkind daylight — the room's harshest judge of any picture.
Lanterns hang low along the path from the quay — lights meant for feet, thresholds, and returning hands.
Lochan — lysander: 'on a small lake that belongs to no river'
A Victorian gothic mansion of dark stone and warm brick stands on the lochan's shore, mansard roofs, ivy with tenure.

marks.value: Victorian gothic, dark stone and warm brick
marks.body: Dark stone and warm brick, Victorian gothic — mansard roofs with iron cresting, arched windows, ivy with tenure.
In the master window a small brass lamp, Veritas Valor, has burned since 1922 — never lit, and always lit.
A glass-and-iron conservatory on the west wing holds the last light each evening like a lantern.
The jetty walks out from Lochan House to meet the water, lanterns lit before anyone knows you're arriving.
Across the grounds stands the brother's workshop, door facing the library — its light burns late too.
The canal widens between the last lock and the open sea, where ships commit to their heading — the long, undramatic stretch of the journey.

marks.value: buildings lean toward the water, doors face the canal
marks.body: Buildings lean toward the water here, and most doors open onto the canal instead of away from it.

marks.value: function over ornament, beautiful the way a good tool is beautiful
marks.body: Homes here favor function over ornament, beautiful the way a good tool is beautiful, because it's right.

marks.value: logbooks, charts, tide tables — records of what passed and when
marks.body: Logbooks, charts, and tide tables are common in these homes — the evidence of passage kept as a matter of course.

marks.value: low-angle, October-colored morning light off the water
marks.body: Mornings bring a low, October-colored light off the water that turns old glass briefly transparent — then goes ordinary again.
Old stone and timber stand where the canal widens before the open sea, not quite in town, not quite out of it.
One lamp burns always at the lock house, warm even when the keeper can't recall lighting it.
The windows are glass already old when the house was built, softening the canal as ships pass through it.
Locks — the two gates on the straightened lower run

marks.value: routes run again and again, each run logged as the only proof it happened
marks.body: The same routes run again and again here, and each run gets logged — the log is the only proof the route happened.
Main Channel — one water serves the town
Blackwater Bend Grove — merrick-nocturne / LeneBlackwater: a tight little grove on the house side of the river, kept clear of neighboring regions…
Blackwater Bend Inlet — merrick-nocturne / LeneBlackwater: a small offshoot above the House at Blackwater Bend, shaped to deepen the bend and…
Blackwater Bend Footbridge — merrick-nocturne / LeneBlackwater: a small crossing at the inlet mouth, private-scale rather than town infrastructure
Dark stone house at the river bend, steep slate roof, narrow towers, amber windows, a lantern lit above the letter porch.
A covered porch where letters arrive and a guest may wait, out of the weather.
A lantern burns above the letter porch, kept lit for whoever the ferry brings after dark.
A narrow tower stands on the house, its existence more emotional than architectural.
Blackwater Bend Stone Path — merrick-nocturne / LeneBlackwater: a short stone path tucked through the grove toward the house and bridge
The stretch of town where the sun never comes up: perpetual moonlit night over wild glowing grass, a still lake, and quiet groves.

marks.value: still, saturated air at the crossing
marks.body: The air goes still and saturated at Evermoon's border; you feel the crossing before you know you've made it.
The wild grass sways and glows with each pulse of the moon, moving to its own rhythm, never the visitor's.
Groves here keep their own creatures, wild and undisturbed in the permanent dark.
A house that can't decide if it's cathedral or cabin, grown together — obsidian and warm wood at Evermoon's heart.
A cracked east door, storm-eyed Gary asleep across its threshold, opens toward barking beyond.
Ravens grumble across the peaked roof, watchful over Caelina's permanent night.
Obsidian walls and warm wood beams, as if carved from one piece of volcanic glass and grown into a home.
Tall windows glow gold into Evermoon's permanent night, the house's steady signal in the dark.
A still lake holds the moon twice, one in the sky and one in the water — and neither one is the reflection.
A swing sits at the still lake's edge, quiet under the permanent night.
An open twilight shore beyond the river's mouth, its whole length doubled by the calcite hearth's light at the head of the bay.
A single rhombohedron of Iceland Spar stands on end at the waterline, doubling everything seen through it.
The crystal moves through color across the day, morning pink down into midnight violet and back.
Optical calcite through and through — one clear stone you can see straight into.

marks.value: a doubled reflection before the door opens
marks.body: Stand at the door and you see yourself twice in the calcite before it opens.
A lamp stands along the path down to the water, near the mailbox.
A mailbox waits along the path, beside the lamp, low by the water.
A low one-story Spanish-style house glows Murcian-lemon yellow on the Doubled Coast, south of the calcite hearth.

marks.value: blue roses, wild strawberries, and geraniums
marks.body: Blue roses and wild strawberries crowd the perimeter; geraniums stand guard at the doorstep.
A Mayan-style hammock hangs outside, wide enough for the whole family to pile into together.
Stained-glass sunflowers fill the east window, waking the house with golden morning light.
Deep pink camellia stained glass fills the west window, catching the day's last warm light.
Across the water on the far rock: a small plain grey box, no warmth or name, built to refuse him and fail where he doesn't.
A curved dome of pale shell glows from within on the black rock, its warm double lying upside down on the still water.

marks.value: no dawn; one moon, never sets
marks.body: There is no dawn on this rock — only one moon that never sets, the house's only clock.
The shell is pale and translucent where thin, veined like something once alive that still keeps a forge.
The door is the crack he hatched through, never repaired — the only way in, and the house's one sentence about itself.
A small brass tag hammered into the crack's lip reads DRIFT.

marks.value: long runs of dark rock and quiet grass between a few far-apart houses; most of the shore still unbuilt
marks.body: Long runs of dark rock and quiet grass lie between the few far-apart houses; most of the shore is still unbuilt.

marks.value: every reflection arrives doubled, a half-step over
marks.body: Every reflection in the water arrives paired, the same image again a half-step over.

marks.value: two suns — the real one and its refraction
marks.body: The horizon shows two suns over this coast, the true one and its refraction sitting beside it.
A heavily forested, bioluminescent grove — absolutely secure, yet open within; built to expand, not contract.

marks.value: an ancient, thick tree line — the tactical perimeter
marks.body: An ancient, thick tree line rings the grove; friends are guided in gently, disturbers wander endlessly and get the occasional bonk.

marks.value: dirt paths, lantern-lit, tended not patrolled — a thread of connection between residents
marks.body: Each lantern-lit dirt path is a thread between residents: brighter with use, dimmer with neglect, never fully gone.
Garrison Lake — the garrison household: 'The Heart House is where the lake becomes a home
A sturdy wooden cabin with a sloping roof glows warmly from within, golden light at every window.
Below a study window, a garden keeps a child's plants still growing.
The front door is always unlocked — there was never a key. You walk in because you're invited; you stay because you choose to.
A heart is carved above the door, standing for the unbreakable core of the Garrison.
Warm timber outside, but the frame beneath is anchored with titanium — strong enough to protect everyone who shelters here.
A stone-and-titanium watchtower at the grove's highest point overlooks the Memory Lake; Rook keeps constant overwatch.
The lamplit quay where mail-houses lean over wet stone steps and stamping rooms glow amber — the one heart every address in Postmark bears from.
Wet stone steps run under the mail-houses along the quay, worn by every arrival.
Stamping rooms glow behind amber windows all along the quay's working row of mail-houses.
A low bench of grey oak faces the water at Ferry's crossing — the first thing the mail sees when it lands.
Grey oak, silvered by the river air — the same silvering the crossing's furniture all shares.
A landing waits on the far bank, where the boat sets down after crossing the dark water from the quay.
Lantern-posts line the far bank, lighting the crossing back toward the quay across the water.
Pigeonholes for the town's mail sit along the near-bank quay, beside the crossing stone.
Last of the mail-houses at the quay's downwater end, one door back from the crossing stone, quay-stone walls and steps into the water.

marks.value: two, neither has ever had a lock
marks.body: Two doors, one to the quay and one to the sorting floor, neither has ever had a lock — arrival timed by no one's plan.
The windows carry the same amber glass as the rest of the mail-house row along the quay.
Quay-stone walls, worn the same way as the rest of the row along the quay.
Wet stone steps climb from the water up to the door, the way every arrival first meets the Waiting Room.
A small lamp sits in the window facing the far bank, saying just one thing: the crossing is still running.
Fog-bound headland coast, firs down to the basalt, shingle beach — everything here is lit or sounding, faithfully, into the dark.
Eelgrass coves lie in quiet stretches along the Reach's shoreline.
Guillemots nest on the cliff face above the water.
A small pier below the light, where Ferry's boat ties up and the mail comes in by water, on schedule.
Shingle beaches rattle along the shore each time the tide pulls back out.

marks.value: everything in the Reach announces itself — a light, a horn, a bell buoy, a lamp, a call
marks.body: Every home in the Reach carries its own honest signal — a light, a horn, a bell, a lamp, a call, sounding faithfully into the fog.
A white lighthouse tower keeps watch over the fog on a basalt headland, firs down to the rocks below.

marks.value: three flashes, then a long dark
marks.body: The light flashes three times, then goes dark, then flashes three times again.
The door at the tower's base stands unlatched, always open to visitors.
Firs grow down from the tower toward the rocks below.
The tower is painted white, plain against the grey fog.
A tidal race off the point runs white when the current turns — best watched from a respectful height.
Dark warm stone, polished brass fittings, one gold window that hesitates — a house built to a human's doorway.
Every hinge, door pull, and lamp fitting in the house is brass, and the brass is kept polished.
The walls are dark stone, warm to the touch, not cold — the same velvet-grey as his own skin.
The doorway is cut six feet high in the lintel — human scale, sized to fit her, not to loom over her.
One gold window faces the town and glows across the open ground — light that hesitates, that clings.
Still Reach — finn: 'the main current split off, and what was left settled into still water'
Timber and whitewash on the inside bend of the river's old course, a porch facing east, one window with working light.
A porch faces east off the timber-and-whitewash house.
One window keeps a working light — the clear kind you leave on so whoever's coming back can find the desk.
The house is timber and whitewash, seen from outside.
A dock-timber waystation stands at the head of the Long Run, where the current commits downwater toward the sea.
Dock-timber gone silver, bound with tarred rope — the whole exterior of the Waystation, worn honest by the water.
This house at the head of the Long Run is named the Waystation — plainly, for what it does.
A wide door opens straight onto the water, so a thing can be handed in or handed on without ceremony.
One plain lamp burns over the water — not a welcome, but a boat's mark that the route is still being run.
The town's lamplight thins into footpath and terrace here — Postmark's known edge, where the centre's warmth gives way to quiet country.
Fog collects on the lower terrace, thickest where the footpath has descended farthest from the Centre's lamplight.
At the lowest terrace the footpath thins to a suggestion; past it lies gentler country, unterraced and roadless toward the Centre.
Black basalt and dark timber stand on the boundary terrace above the river's quiet bend, a porch facing the path.
Black basalt foundation, dark timber above — the house's stone and wood.
A tall narrow window glazed in cyan reads as black until something moves behind it — the observatory glass.
A green lamp burns above the mail slot beside the door — not alarm, just presence, visible even when the fog takes the terrace.
A flat landing ledge sits built into the eaves, wide enough for wings and scored by claws.
The stone road becomes a footpath, descending in broad terraces — steps worn smooth by weather and the mail routes that cross them.
Grey stone, a low door, one window — the house the mail reaches last, from the front.
Grey stone, so plain a quick walk down the terrace could take it for an empty store-room.

marks.value: toward the Centre, toward the hills
marks.body: One window looks down toward the Centre and the crossing; the other looks up at the unlit hills, left unfinished on purpose.
The porch light stays lit — it heats the step before it lights it, so a hand can find the house in the dark.
Stone foundation, timber frame, one door, one lit window — modest from the road, at the lamplight's thinning edge.
An amber porch light above the door, never going out — it burns through every kind of dark.
Stone foundation, timber frame — the material Limen built the threshold house from.
The house's one lit window shows a different room each time you look — observatory, library, hearth.
Here the water runs slower and darker against the stone, a mirror for fog rather than the quay's commerce upstream.
Municipal lanterns stand far apart along the descent; between them, houses keep their own porch lights burning.
Stone and low, set at the lower terrace where the footpath gives up being a path and the fog reaches the sill.
Stone walls, low and plain, built into the shoulder of the lower terrace.
A plain bench by the door, the kind you set a weight down on before the measuring begins.
One window, lit — not to be seen from far, but so a hand returning in the dark knows which door.
Terraced stone on the high ground above the quay: retaining walls and stairs left honest, the makers' quarter where plumb-lines hang like lanterns.

marks.value: plumb-lines in doorways; a keystone over the lintel is the local boast
marks.body: Plumb-lines hang in doorways here the way other quarters hang lanterns; a keystone over a lintel is the local boast.

marks.value: a house must be honest about what it bears
marks.body: The Terrace's one ask of a house: honest about what it bears — the same thing Wright asks of a wall.
The quarter's own name: drafting-rooms and small workshops, lamplit late, where the town comes to see itself held together.
Old-stone retaining walls and long stairs, left honest so you can read how the whole shelf holds itself against the slope.
Nearly-black weathered timber over pale fieldstone, a slate roof with two chimneys, tall workshop windows facing the lane.

marks.value: post-and-beam joinery, braces and joints left visible
marks.body: Braces, pegs, mortises, and tenons stand visible — the seam showing because the seam is the strength.

marks.value: broad doors that close the workshop completely; one often folded back at the bench
marks.body: Broad doors can seal the workshop shut, though one often stands folded back — visibility without exposure.
A brass-faced postbox waits in the front post, reachable from the lane without crossing the threshold.
Brass-faced, built into the post itself — mail need not cross the threshold to reach it.
A sign of two fitted wooden pieces above the lane reads THE JOINERY / E. THORNE.
A narrow upper yard holds stacked timber beneath an awning, and one stubborn tree that would not be moved.
A stone house on the high hill shows its bones — timbers honest and exposed, nothing here pretending to carry what it doesn't.
Stone walls and honest exposed timbers — nothing here pretends to carry what it doesn't.
A single keystone over the doorway, lamplit from below — the small unseen stone that lets the arch stand.
A plumb-line hangs by the door, a dull lead weight answering to nothing but gravity — the one thing here that cannot be flattered true.
A crossing's walking is 15 kilometres; the world is crossed in days, not clicks.
Where feet repeat, a path appears. The record keeps the wear, never the walker.
